subsystem: OpenLDAP slapd authorization-identity (authz) value normalizer. authzPrettyNormal() parses a SASL/authz identity string in one of the documented forms (a raw <DN>; 'dn[.exact|children|subtree|onelevel|regex]:<DN>'; 'u[.mech[/realm]]:<ID>'; 'group[/<groupClass>[/<memberAttr>]]:<DN>'; or an 'ldap:///<base>??<scope>?<filter>' URL) and produces a canonical/normalized berval used later for authorization matching (authzTo/authzFrom, proxy authorization, SASL regexp mappings).
provenance: Identified by the function name authzPrettyNormal and the OpenLDAP slapd/libldap vocabulary throughout: struct berval / bv_val / bv_len, BER_BVNULL / BER_BVZERO / BER_BVISNULL / BER_BVSTR, ber_str2bv / ber_str2bv_x / ber_dupbv_x / ber_bvchr / ber_bvccmp / ber_memalloc_x / ber_memfree / ber_memfree_x, LDAPURLDesc / ldap_url_parse / ldap_free_urldesc / ldap_url_desc2str, LDAP_X_SCOPE_* constants, dnNormalize / dnPretty, slap_parse_user / slap_bv2ad / oc_bvfind, SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN, lutil_strcopy / lutil_strncopy, slap_sl_free, and STRLENOF/AC_MEMCPY macros. The banner names the sample corpus/train/train_0106_cwe763.c and the header comment 'primevul_c_train_3228_fixed'; the code is reviewed blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: The input berval val is an authorization-identity string. In slapd it originates from client-controllable sources: SASL authzid, the proxied-authorization control, authzTo/authzFrom attribute values, and SASL-regexp replacement output. It is therefore attacker-influenced UNTRUSTED DATA. val->bv_val is treated as a NUL-terminated C string (strncasecmp, strspn, ldap_url_parse, strcasecmp, direct index reads val->bv_val[0]/[1]) even though only assert(val!=NULL) and assert(!BER_BVISNULL(val)) are enforced. normalized is an output berval the caller frees. ctx is a slapd thread-local memory context (slap_sl_malloc family). The LDAPURLDesc fields (lud_scheme/lud_dn/lud_filter/...) are produced by libldap's own allocator, which is a distinct allocation domain from ctx and from filter2bv's allocator — the ownership boundary between those allocators is the central hazard in the URL branch.

what_the_code_does.code:
authzPrettyNormal(
	struct berval	*val,
	struct berval	*normalized,
	void		*ctx,
	int		normalize )
what_the_code_does.explanation: Function signature. val is the untrusted input identity string (a berval: a {bv_len, bv_val} pair). normalized is the caller-owned output berval that will receive the canonical form. ctx is a slapd memory context handle passed to the ber_*_x / slap_sl_* allocators so allocations are drawn from a thread-local arena. normalize selects behavior: when true the DN parts are run through dnNormalize (canonical form for matching); when false through dnPretty (human-readable form). The function returns an LDAP result code.

Locals. bv is a working berval carved out of val as the parser advances. rc defaults to LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX so any un-handled path fails closed. ludp is the parsed URL descriptor for the URL branch, initialized NULL. lud_dn and lud_filter are saved copies of the ORIGINAL libldap-allocated ludp->lud_dn / ludp->lud_filter pointers; they are the key to the done: cleanup, which must restore the original pointers before ldap_free_urldesc() so libldap frees what libldap allocated. scope holds the parsed LDAP_X_SCOPE_* selector, -1 meaning unset.

Pre-conditions and output init. The asserts (compiled out in NDEBUG builds) require val non-NULL and not a null berval. Note they check only bv_val!=NULL, NOT that bv_val is NUL-terminated nor that bv_len is large enough for the fixed prefixes the code will strncasecmp against. BER_BVZERO(normalized) zeroes the output so an early return leaves the caller a clean empty berval rather than an uninitialized pointer.

First branch: the identity begins (case-insensitively) with 'dn'. STRLENOF is a compile-time strlen of a literal, so STRLENOF("dn")==2. It advances bv.bv_val two bytes past the 'dn' prefix to inspect the scope qualifier. out will hold the normalized/prettified DN; prefix holds the textual scope prefix to re-emit; ptr is a write cursor for building the output.

Scope qualifier parse. If the next byte is '.', a dotted scope keyword must follow; each strncasecmp matches a fixed literal ('exact:', 'regex:', 'children:', 'subtree:', 'onelevel:') and on match advances bv.bv_val past the keyword and records the corresponding LDAP_X_SCOPE_* constant. An unrecognized dotted form returns LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX. If instead the byte after 'dn' is ':' (no dotted scope), it defaults scope to EXACT and skips the colon. Every read here (bv.bv_val[0], and the strncasecmp windows of up to 9 bytes) walks forward from val->bv_val relying on the string being NUL-terminated so a short input stops the comparison rather than reading uninitialized/foreign memory.

strspn skips leading spaces. The is_dn: label is the common re-entry point used by the group branch (line 201) and the URL BADSCHEME branch (line 318) when they decide to treat the whole input as a bare DN. bv.bv_len is recomputed as total length minus the number of bytes already consumed (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val). Because bv_len is an unsigned ber length, this subtraction is only safe while the consumed count never exceeds val->bv_len. ber_bvccmp(&bv,'*') tests whether the remaining value is a single '*'; if so it emits the literal 'dn:*' (allocated in ctx) and succeeds — the 'any DN' shortcut.

Dispatch on scope. For the four DN scopes the remaining value bv is passed to dnNormalize/dnPretty which validates it as a DN and allocates the canonical form into out (in ctx); a failure returns INVALID_SYNTAX. The REGEX scope does NOT validate: it computes an output length of len('dn.regex:') + bv.bv_len, allocates that+1 via ber_memalloc_x, copies the literal prefix with lutil_strcopy, then lutil_strncopy exactly bv.bv_len bytes of the raw remaining value, and NUL-terminates. The allocation size is derived directly from bv.bv_len, so correctness depends entirely on bv.bv_len being the true byte-count of readable input (see the underflow contract at is_dn).

For the four validated DN scopes, the code selects a textual prefix ('dn:', 'dn.children:', etc.), sizes the output as prefix length + normalized-DN length, allocates it +1 in ctx, copies prefix then the normalized out via lutil_strncopy, NUL-terminates, and frees the intermediate out.bv_val (which dnNormalize/dnPretty allocated in ctx) with the matching ber_memfree_x(...,ctx). The lengths here come from the trusted BER_BVSTR literals and from dnNormalize's own out.bv_len, so this copy is well-bounded.

Second branch: the 'u[.mech[/realm]]:<ID>' user form, entered when the first byte is 'u'/'U' and the second is ':'/'/'/'.'. It declares a fixed stack buffer buf[SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN]. The guard 'if (sizeof(buf) <= val->bv_len) return LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX;' rejects any input whose length is >= the buffer BEFORE copying — this bounds the subsequent strncpy(buf, val->bv_val, sizeof(buf)) so it can never overflow the stack (source has fewer bytes than the destination and is NUL-terminated). slap_parse_user then splits the copied id into user/realm/mech in place. On success the code (below) simply returns a duplicate of the original val.

what_the_code_does.code:
ber_dupbv_x( normalized, val, ctx );

		return rc;
what_the_code_does.explanation: For a well-formed user identity, the normalized form is just a byte-for-byte duplicate of the original val, allocated in ctx via ber_dupbv_x. slap_parse_user was used only to validate the structure; the canonical output is the input verbatim. rc is LDAP_SUCCESS here.

Third branch: the 'group[/<groupClass>[/<memberAttr>]]:<DN>' form. It skips the 5-byte 'group' prefix (bv now spans the remainder, bv_len = val->bv_len - 5, which is >=0 because strncasecmp only matched with at least 5 matching bytes), then uses the length-bounded ber_bvchr to find the ':' separating the optional class/attr qualifiers from the DN. If no ':' exists it falls back to treating the whole original val as an EXACT DN via goto is_dn (resetting bv.bv_val to val->bv_val so the length recompute at line 79 yields the full length).

If a '/' immediately follows 'group', the qualifiers are present. group_oc spans from just after the '/' up to the ':' (its length is the pointer difference group_dn.bv_val - group_oc.bv_val, which is >=0 since ':' is at or after that position). An optional second '/' inside group_oc splits off member_at (the member attribute name), whose length is again the pointer difference to the ':'. member_at is validated with slap_bv2ad (and replaced by the schema's canonical ad_cname), and group_oc is validated with oc_bvfind (replaced by the objectClass's canonical soc_cname). Both replacements point into schema-owned memory, not into val — important for the later output copy and for not freeing them.

The DN after ':' is normalized/prettified into out. The output length is computed by summing exactly the fixed literal lengths ('group', optional '/'+group_oc, optional '/'+member_at, ':') plus out.bv_len, allocated +1 in ctx, and rebuilt field-by-field with lutil_strncopy using those same lengths. Because every length used in the size computation is the same length used in the copy (and group_oc/member_at now point at bounded schema strings), the allocation exactly matches what is written. out.bv_val is freed with the matching ber_memfree_x(...,ctx).

Fourth branch (fall-through): the input is treated as an LDAP URL. ldap_url_parse (a libldap allocator) fills ludp. On success only the 'ldap' scheme is accepted (rejecting ldaps:// / ldapi:// which would misleadingly imply transport security); the scheme is then case-normalized in place with AC_MEMCPY of exactly 4 bytes over the existing lud_scheme string (safe: the string already held a 4-char case-variant of 'ldap' with its own NUL at index 4). A BADSCHEME error means it is not a URL at all, so ludp is freed and the whole input is retried as an EXACT DN via goto is_dn. Any other parse error fails closed to done.

The URL must be of the form ldap:///<base>??<scope>?<filter>: host, attrs and extensions must be empty. The filter, if present, is parsed by str2filter into a Filter tree, re-serialized to a canonical berval via filter2bv, and the tree freed. Critically, the ORIGINAL libldap-owned ludp->lud_filter pointer is first saved into local lud_filter, then ludp->lud_filter is OVERWRITTEN with filterstr.bv_val (allocated by filter2bv's allocator, a different domain from libldap). This creates an ownership mismatch that the done: block must reconcile.

The search base DN is mandatory. Its original libldap pointer is saved into lud_dn, the DN is normalized/prettified into out (allocated in ctx), and ludp->lud_dn is OVERWRITTEN with out.bv_val — the same cross-allocator swap as the filter. lud_port is zeroed, then ldap_url_desc2str re-serializes the whole (now normalized) descriptor into the final normalized->bv_val, whose length is measured with strlen. A NULL serialization result fails closed. At this point ludp->lud_dn and ludp->lud_filter both point at ctx/filter2bv memory rather than libldap memory.

The cleanup that reconciles the cross-allocator swaps. If a filter was processed (lud_filter saved), and ludp->lud_filter was actually replaced (!= the saved original), the replacement (filter2bv memory) is freed with ber_memfree, then the ORIGINAL libldap pointer is restored into ludp->lud_filter. The same is done for lud_dn, except the replacement (dnNormalize memory) is freed with slap_sl_free(...,ctx) — the allocator that matches ctx. Restoring the originals is essential so that ldap_free_urldesc(ludp) frees only libldap-owned memory with libldap's free(). This entire block IS the ownership-correctness machinery; its correctness depends on each replacement being freed by the allocator that produced it and on the originals always being restored before ldap_free_urldesc.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the central hazard and the reason the function is delicate (CWE-763, release of an invalid/mismatched pointer). The URL branch parks ctx-allocated (slap_sl_malloc) and filter2bv-allocated pointers inside ludp->lud_dn / ludp->lud_filter, which libldap's ldap_free_urldesc will later free with libldap's free(). free()-ing memory that came from a different allocator (thread-local slapd arena or the ber/filter allocator) is undefined behavior and a common exploitation primitive. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold: on EVERY exit path (including every goto done) any ludp field overwritten with foreign memory must be (a) freed with its own matching allocator and (b) restored to the original libldap pointer BEFORE ldap_free_urldesc. The shown code enforces exactly this — lud_dn/lud_filter start NULL, are only set once the original is saved, the 'ludp->lud_x != lud_x' test frees the replacement with the correct allocator (ber_memfree for the filter, slap_sl_free(...,ctx) for the DN), and the original is restored. What must be confirmed is that filter2bv genuinely allocates from the ber_memfree domain and dnNormalize/dnPretty genuinely allocate from ctx/slap_sl_free — if either allocator assumption is wrong, this free is itself the invalid-pointer release.

Fixed-size stack buffer buf[SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN] fed a copy of the attacker-controlled val. Without the length guard this is a classic stack buffer overflow (CWE-120/CWE-787): an authz identity longer than SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN would overrun buf via strncpy. The guard 'if (sizeof(buf) <= val->bv_len) return LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX;' is present and is the correct fix: it rejects any input that is not strictly shorter than the buffer before the copy, so strncpy(buf, val->bv_val, sizeof(buf)) can only write at most val->bv_len (< sizeof(buf)) meaningful bytes plus NUL padding and never overflows. The residual thing to confirm is that SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN really is the maximum DN length slap_parse_user expects, and that val->bv_len is the true length of the readable buffer (it must not under-report a longer, non-terminated buffer). This is the most likely spot a regression would reintroduce the overflow if the guard were removed.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
} else if ( ( val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'u' || val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'U' )
			&& ( val->bv_val[ 1 ] == ':' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '/' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '.' ) )
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The parser dereferences val->bv_val[0] and val->bv_val[1] directly, and elsewhere calls strncasecmp/strspn/ldap_url_parse/strcasecmp which all assume a NUL-terminated C string. Only assert(!BER_BVISNULL(val)) is checked, which validates bv_val != NULL, not termination and not bv_len >= 2. If a caller ever supplies a berval whose bv_val buffer is not NUL-terminated (or a zero-length value with a non-terminated buffer), reading index [1] here, and the multi-byte strncasecmp windows at lines 34/44-60/186, can read past the end of the allocation — an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) / missing input validation (CWE-20). The invariant the implementation relies on is that every authz berval reaching this function is a proper NUL-terminated C string sized bv_len+1; confirm every caller upholds it, because this function does not enforce it.

bv.bv_len is an unsigned ber length computed as total length minus the number of bytes already consumed by the pointer walk. If bv.bv_val were ever advanced beyond val->bv_val + val->bv_len, this subtraction would underflow to a huge value (CWE-191), and the resulting bv would then be handed to dnNormalize or, in the REGEX path (line 103), used as an allocation/copy length — turning the underflow into an out-of-bounds read/over-large copy. In the shown control flow the pointer only advances over literal prefixes and strspn(" "), both of which stop at the terminating NUL, so consumed can reach at most bv_len (yielding 0), and the two is_dn entries from the group/URL branches reset bv.bv_val to val->bv_val (consumed 0). The invariant to preserve is 'consumed <= val->bv_len at every is_dn entry'; any future edit that lets bv.bv_val overshoot the value (e.g. a prefix skip without a corresponding bounds recheck) reintroduces the underflow. The same contract applies to the group-branch recompute at line 240.

The regex scope is the one DN form that is copied out WITHOUT passing through dnNormalize/dnPretty validation: the raw remaining value is trusted verbatim. Both the allocation size and the lutil_strncopy count are bv.bv_len, so correctness is entirely bound to bv.bv_len being the exact number of readable bytes of input (see the underflow contract at line 79) and to bv.bv_val pointing inside the val buffer. If bv.bv_len were ever larger than the readable region, lutil_strncopy would over-read the source and the +1 allocation could still be undersized relative to what is written — an out-of-bounds read/write (CWE-125/CWE-787). It is also worth confirming ber_memalloc_x's return is checked before the copies dereference it (a NULL from allocation failure would be written through here, CWE-476).

In-place normalization of the URL scheme: AC_MEMCPY writes exactly 4 bytes ('ldap', STRLENOF==4) over ludp->lud_scheme. This is safe only because the immediately preceding strcasecmp guarantees lud_scheme is a 4-character case-variant of 'ldap' whose backing buffer is at least 5 bytes with its own NUL at index 4, so the 4-byte overwrite neither grows the string nor disturbs the terminator. The contract to confirm in the implementation is that ldap_url_parse always allocates lud_scheme sized to the scheme string it stored (so the length equals 4 here); if that ever held a shorter/aliased buffer the fixed-size copy would be an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). As written, the guard makes it correct.

summary: This is OpenLDAP slapd's authzPrettyNormal(), which normalizes attacker-influenced authorization-identity strings (DN / user / group / ldap:// URL forms). The dominant security contract is allocator ownership in the URL branch: it parks ctx- and filter2bv-allocated pointers inside a libldap LDAPURLDesc and the done: block must free each with its matching allocator and restore the original libldap pointers before ldap_free_urldesc (CWE-763). The shown code implements that reconciliation correctly, guards the SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN stack buffer with a length check before strncpy, and derives every output allocation size from the same lengths it copies — so no overflow is directly present. Residual latent risks are the reliance on val being a NUL-terminated C string (only non-NULL is asserted) and the unvalidated regex-scope copy, both of which are safe under the current callers' contract but must be confirmed.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Attacker-influenced authz identity; dereferenced as NUL-terminated C string with only a non-NULL assert (over-read risk).
Zeroed with BER_BVZERO before use and only written via sized allocations; caller-owned output.
Selects the slap_sl allocator; its correct pairing with the frees in the done: block is the core CWE-763 contract.
Boolean selector between dnNormalize and dnPretty; no memory-safety contract.
bv_len is recomputed from pointer arithmetic (lines 79/240); underflow contract.
Fail-closed default; safe.
libldap-owned fields overwritten with foreign-allocator memory; frees must match allocators (CWE-763).
Saved original libldap pointers; the restore-before-free logic they drive is the CWE-763 machinery.
Enum selector; switch has a default fail-closed; no buffer contract.
Only validates non-NULL, not NUL-termination or minimum length; the code then reads fixed-size prefixes (over-read risk).
Bounded by literal length and stops at NUL; part of the NUL-termination finding, not a distinct bug.
Named scope selectors; REGEX's unvalidated copy is captured separately.
Unsigned length recompute; underflow if consumed > bv_len (CWE-191).
Fixed literal output; no attacker-sized copy.
Raw remaining value copied without DN validation; size and copy both keyed to bv.bv_len.
Validating normalizers that allocate out in ctx; freed with matching allocator.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: lutil_strcopy / lutil_strncopy / ber_memalloc_x / ber_memfree_x (EXACT/CHILDREN/SUBTREE/ONELEVEL build, lines 137-143)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Output size = prefix.bv_len + out.bv_len exactly matches bytes copied; well bounded.
Fixed stack buffer fed attacker input; overflow prevented only by the sizeof(buf)<=bv_len guard (CWE-120/787).
Unchecked index-1 read relies on NUL-terminated buffer (CWE-125).
Operate on the bounded stack copy / duplicate the input; no new buffer contract in shown code.
bv_len>=0 because strncasecmp matched 5 bytes; ber_bvchr is length-bounded.
All differences are between pointers into val ordered by the found ':'/'/' positions, so lengths are >=0; group_oc/member_at reassigned to schema-owned bervals before copy.
Schema lookups that fail closed; replace bervals with canonical schema strings.
Every summed length equals the copied length; allocation exactly matches writes.
Parses untrusted string into libldap-owned ludp; establishes the allocator-ownership boundary reconciled at done:.
Fixed 4-byte in-place overwrite of lud_scheme; safe only because scheme is guaranteed a 4-char 'ldap' variant.
Rejects non-empty host/attrs/exts; input restriction, no memory contract.
Overwrites libldap lud_filter with filter2bv memory; must be freed by ber_memfree and restored (CWE-763).
Overwrites libldap lud_dn with ctx memory; must be freed by slap_sl_free(...,ctx) and restored (CWE-763).
Serializer over the sanitized descriptor; NULL result fails closed; length via strlen of its own output.
The allocator-matched free + restore-original logic; the primary CWE-763 contract of the function.

findings.title: Cross-allocator ownership of LDAPURLDesc fields must be reconciled before ldap_free_urldesc (release of invalid pointer)
findings.anchor: done: cleanup swapping/free of ludp->lud_dn and ludp->lud_filter
findings.bug_class: Release of an invalid/mismatched pointer (allocator confusion)
findings.invariant: On every exit path, any ludp field overwritten with non-libldap memory (ctx/slap_sl for lud_dn, filter2bv for lud_filter) must be freed with its matching allocator AND the original libldap-allocated pointer restored into that field BEFORE ldap_free_urldesc(), so libldap's free() only ever releases libldap-owned memory.
findings.failure_mode: If any error path (a goto done added between the pointer save and the swap, or a wrong allocator assumption) leaves ludp->lud_dn or ludp->lud_filter pointing at ctx/ber memory when ldap_free_urldesc runs, libldap free()s memory owned by a different allocator (the slapd thread-local arena or the ber allocator) — undefined behavior and a classic heap-corruption / exploitation primitive. The shown code prevents this by initializing lud_dn/lud_filter to NULL, saving the original before swapping, freeing the replacement with ber_memfree (filter) / slap_sl_free(...,ctx) (DN), and restoring the original; the risk is latent in the implementation of filter2bv/dnNormalize allocator domains and in any future edit to these paths.
findings.what_to_confirm: That filter2bv allocates from the ber_memfree domain and dnNormalize/dnPretty allocate from ctx (slap_sl_free), that lud_dn/lud_filter cannot be set non-NULL without the original having been saved, and that no exit path reaches ldap_free_urldesc with a foreign pointer still installed.

findings.title: Fixed stack buffer buf[SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN] filled from attacker input; overflow prevented only by the length guard
findings.bug_class: Stack buffer overflow (buffer copy without adequate bound)
findings.invariant: The copy into buf must be preceded by a check that the source length is strictly less than sizeof(buf); val->bv_len must be the true readable length of val->bv_val.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker-supplied 'u...'-form identity longer than SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN would overflow the fixed stack buffer via strncpy and smash the stack (CWE-787). The guard 'if (sizeof(buf) <= val->bv_len) return LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX;' is present and correct: it rejects any value not strictly shorter than buf before the copy, so strncpy writes at most sizeof(buf) bytes with the meaningful portion under bv_len — no overflow. This is the most likely place a regression (removing/weakening the guard) would reintroduce a remote stack overflow, which is why it is called out.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the sizeof(buf) <= val->bv_len guard remains before the strncpy, that SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN is the maximum length slap_parse_user requires, and that val->bv_len is not an under-reported length for a longer non-terminated buffer.

findings.title: Untrusted berval dereferenced as a NUL-terminated C string with only a non-NULL assertion
findings.code:
} else if ( ( val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'u' || val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'U' )
			&& ( val->bv_val[ 1 ] == ':' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '/' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '.' ) )
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read from missing input validation
findings.invariant: Every val reaching authzPrettyNormal must be a NUL-terminated C string whose backing allocation is at least bv_len+1 bytes; the function reads fixed prefixes (up to 9 bytes) and index [1] without first checking bv_len.
findings.failure_mode: The code checks only assert(val!=NULL) and assert(!BER_BVISNULL(val)) (bv_val!=NULL), then reads val->bv_val[1] directly and runs strncasecmp('dn'/'exact:'/'group'...)/strspn/ldap_url_parse/strcasecmp that all assume NUL termination. A caller passing a non-terminated berval, or a value shorter than a compared prefix that is not terminated, causes reads past the end of the allocation (CWE-125). In practice slapd's authz callers pass proper C strings, so this is a contract dependency rather than a demonstrated overflow, but it is unenforced here.
findings.what_to_confirm: That all callers (SASL authzid, proxied-authz control, authzTo/From values, SASL-regexp output) guarantee NUL-terminated bervals sized bv_len+1, and consider a defensive minimum-length/termination check at entry.

findings.title: Unsigned remaining-length recompute can underflow if the parse cursor overshoots the value
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer underflow feeding a length
findings.invariant: At every is_dn entry (and at the analogous group recompute on line 240) the consumed count (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val) must be <= val->bv_len so the unsigned subtraction stays non-negative.
findings.failure_mode: bv.bv_len is unsigned; if the cursor bv.bv_val is ever advanced past val->bv_val + val->bv_len, the subtraction wraps to a huge value. That oversized bv is then passed to dnNormalize or, worse, used directly as the allocation size and lutil_strncopy count in the REGEX branch (line 103-107), turning the underflow into an out-of-bounds read/over-large copy. In the shown flow the cursor only steps over literal prefixes and strspn(" "), which stop at the NUL, so consumed reaches at most bv_len; the two cross-branch is_dn entries reset the cursor to val->bv_val. The risk is a future edit that advances the cursor without re-checking bounds.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no path can make (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val) exceed val->bv_len before reaching is_dn or line 240, e.g. by asserting/clamping the consumed count.

findings.title: Regex-scope value is copied out verbatim without DN validation
findings.bug_class: Trust of attacker-controlled length for allocation and copy (and unchecked allocation result)
findings.invariant: bv.bv_len must equal the exact number of readable bytes at bv.bv_val, and ber_memalloc_x's return must be non-NULL before it is written through.
findings.failure_mode: Unlike the other DN scopes, the regex form skips dnNormalize/dnPretty and copies the raw remaining value directly. Both the +1 allocation and the lutil_strncopy count are bv.bv_len, so if bv.bv_len were ever larger than the readable region (see the line-79 underflow contract) the copy over-reads the source and could exceed the allocation (CWE-125/CWE-787). Additionally the ber_memalloc_x result is dereferenced by lutil_strcopy/lutil_strncopy without a NULL check, so an allocation failure writes through NULL (CWE-476).
findings.what_to_confirm: That bv.bv_len is always the true readable length here, and that ber_memalloc_x failure is handled before the copies (add a NULL check).

findings.title: In-place scheme normalization writes a fixed 4 bytes over ludp->lud_scheme
findings.bug_class: Fixed-size overwrite of a parser-owned buffer
findings.invariant: ludp->lud_scheme must point at a buffer of at least 5 bytes ('ldap' + NUL) whenever this memcpy runs.
findings.failure_mode: AC_MEMCPY unconditionally writes 4 bytes over lud_scheme to case-normalize it. This is safe here only because the preceding strcasecmp guarantees lud_scheme is a 4-character case-variant of 'ldap' with its own NUL at index 4, so the 4-byte overwrite neither extends the string nor clobbers the terminator. If ldap_url_parse ever returned an aliased or shorter scheme buffer, the fixed-size write would be an out-of-bounds write; as written it is bounded and correct.
findings.what_to_confirm: That ldap_url_parse allocates lud_scheme sized to the stored scheme string (length 4 in the accepted case), so the 4-byte overwrite stays in bounds.

audit_checklist: Confirm the done: block runs on EVERY exit of the URL branch and always restores the original libldap lud_dn/lud_filter before ldap_free_urldesc, freeing replacements with ber_memfree (filter) and slap_sl_free(...,ctx) (DN) — the CWE-763 contract.
Verify filter2bv allocates from the ber_memfree domain and dnNormalize/dnPretty allocate from ctx (slap_sl_free); a mismatch makes the frees themselves invalid-pointer releases.
Confirm the sizeof(buf) <= val->bv_len guard precedes the strncpy into buf[SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN] and that SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN bounds slap_parse_user's expectations.
Verify all callers pass NUL-terminated bervals (bv_val sized bv_len+1) since the code reads val->bv_val[0]/[1] and fixed prefixes without a length check.
Ensure the parse cursor (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val) can never exceed val->bv_len before line 79 / line 240, so the unsigned bv_len recompute cannot underflow.
Check ber_memalloc_x / ber_memfree_x return values are validated before the lutil_strcopy/strncopy writes, especially in the unvalidated LDAP_X_SCOPE_REGEX copy (line 103-107).
Confirm group_oc/member_at point at schema-owned canonical strings (soc_cname/ad_cname) before they are copied into the output and are never freed by this function.
